title: Correlated Quantum Dephasometry: Symmetry-Resolved Noise Spectroscopy of Two-Dimensional Superconductors and Altermagnets
abstract: Symmetry-resolved spectroscopies, such as angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy and polarization-resolved Raman, are central for quantum material characterization, yet remain challenging at nanoscale dimensions and low frequencies. Here, we propose correlated quantum dephasometry, which enables symmetry resolved quantum noise spectroscopy of materials at nanoscale and low ($\sim$MHz) frequencies via correlated dephasing of two spin qubits near materials. Our approach leverages the finite-range spatial structures of nonlocal near-field noise correlations to isolate rotational symmetry of the material response in momentum space beyond single qubit capabilities. We apply our approach to two-dimensional (2D) superconductors, and predict clear fingerprints that discriminate s-, d-, and g-wave symmetry of the superconducting gap. To highlight the generality, we further show that the same framework resolves 2D s-wave antiferromagnets and d-wave altermagnets. Our results establish correlated quantum dephasometry as a nanoscale, low-frequency complement for symmetry-resolved spectroscopy applicable to a broad class of quantum materials.
summary: This paper introduces a new technique called correlated quantum dephasometry to perform symmetry-resolved spectroscopy of 2D materials at the nanoscale. By measuring the correlated dephasing of two spin qubits, the method can resolve the angular (rotational) symmetry of material properties like conductivity and magnetic susceptibility. The authors demonstrate that this approach can distinguish between different superconducting pairing symmetries (s, d, and g-wave) and identify altermagnetic structures. This provides a low-frequency, high-resolution complement to traditional high-frequency spectroscopic tools.
main_problem: Traditional symmetry-resolved spectroscopies like ARPES are limited to high frequencies and mesoscopic scales, and single-qubit quantum sensing lacks the ability to resolve angular/rotational symmetry in momentum space.
main_result: The authors propose 'correlated quantum dephasometry,' a method using two spin qubits to resolve the rotational symmetry (even and odd orders) of material responses, such as superconducting gap symmetry and altermagnetic susceptibility, at the nanoscale and low frequencies.
method: The method uses the spatial correlations of near-field noise between two qubits and applies a Jacobi-Anger expansion to perform an angular Fourier analysis of the material response.
model_or_system: The system consists of two spin qubits (e.g., NV centers) positioned at a height z above a 2D material, such as 2D superconductors (modeled via BCS/BdG Hamiltonian) or 2D altermagnets (modeled via Heisenberg/spin diffusion models).
key_observables: Correlated dephasing function (Phi_c), correlated phase rotation function (Psi_c), and Fourier coefficients representing rotational symmetry orders (O_2n and O_2n+1).
important_parameters: Low frequency (MHz), nanoscale spatial scale, interqubit distance (D), qubit height (z), and qubit orientation angle (beta).
main_assumptions: The derivation focuses on the pure dephasing regime (longitudinal coupling) and assumes an extreme near-field regime where the s-polarized evanescent wave dominates.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares the normalized transverse conductivity for s-, d-, and g-wave superconductors and demonstrates the superiority of correlated vs. single-qubit dephasometry in resolving angular symmetry.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitation of current spectroscopy, proposes the correlated dephasometry framework, provides the mathematical derivation for symmetry decomposition using Bessel functions, and demonstrates applications to 2D superconductors and altermagnets.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it develops a new protocol for using multi-qubit sensors to perform momentum-space tomography of environmental noise and material response functions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two-qubit correlated quantum dephasometry enables symmetry-resolved quantum noise spectroscopy by probing spatial noise correlations near quantum materials. (a,b) Single-qubit quantum dephasometry probes local magnetic noise near materials via single qubit dephasing function Φs(α). α is the in-plane orientation of the single qubit. (d,e) Correlated quantum dephasometry probes spatially nonlocal magnetic noise correlations near materials via correlated dephasing function Φc(β). β represents the two-qubit orientation. (c,f) Correlated quantum dephasometry decomposes the angular symmetry of the nonlocal response function O(q, θq) in momentum space into rotational Fourier components of...

FIG. 2. Two-qubit correlated quantum dephasometry isolates the superconductor pairing symmetry. (a-c) Schematic of two spin qubits (Q1, Q2) near 2D superconductors with s-, d-, and g-wave pairing symmetry. The rotational symmetry of transverse conductivity Re σT (q, θq)/σn in momentum space inherits symmetry of gap functions. (d-f) Single-qubit dephasing function Φs(α) exhibits similar behaviors near s-, d-, and g-wave superconductors. (g-i) Correlated dephasing function Φc(β) with respect to the two-qubit orientation β resolves the pairing symmetry for s-, d, and g-wave superconductors. We take D = 8z in (g,h) and D = 12z in (i).

FIG. 3. Two-qubit correlated quantum dephasometry re- solves s-wave antiferromagnets and d-wave altermagnets. (a,b) Schematic of two spin qubits (Q1, Q2) near 2D anti- ferromagnets and altermagnets. The rotational symmetry of magnetic susceptibility Im χN(q, θq) in momentum space in- herits symmetry of magnon band splittings. (c,d) Single-qubit dephasing function Φs(α) exhibits similar behaviors near s-, d-wave magnets. (e,f) Correlated dephasing function Φc(β) exhibits qualitatively distinct angular signatures, directly re- solving the d-wave symmetry of 2D altermagnets. We take D = 9z in (e,f).
